1: Josiah “summoned all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem” — the political assembly precedes the covenantal assembly. The gathering of the elders (ziqnê) as covenant witnesses is the Mosaic pattern: Moses summoned the elders for the Passover institution (Exodus 12:21) and covenant ratification (Exodus 24:1). The covenantal event requires representative witnesses.
2: The king goes to the house of YHWH with all the men of Judah, all the inhabitants of Jerusalem, the priests, the prophets, and all the people from the least to the greatest — lᵉmiqāṭōn wəʿad gādôl. The universality formula marks this as a covenant assembly, not merely a royal ceremony. The king reads “in their ears all the words of the Book of the Covenant” (sēfer habbᵉrît) — the public oral reading of the covenant text, the renewal of the Sinai scene. The reading is loud enough to enter their ears: covenant hearing precedes covenant commitment.
3: “The king stood beside the pillar (ʿāmad ʿal hāʿammûḏ) and renewed the covenant before YHWH.” The pillar/column was the royal standing place in the temple (cf. 2 Kings 11:14; 2 Chronicles 34:31). Josiah’s renewed covenant includes three Deuteronomic elements: (1) lālek ʾaḥar YHWH — to follow/walk after YHWH; (2) lišmōr miṣwōtāyw — to keep his commandments; (3) bəkol lēḇ ûḇəkol nepeš — with all heart and all soul. The last phrase is the beginning of the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:5) applied covenantally. “All the people entered the covenant” (wayyaʿămōḏ kol hāʿām babbᵉrît) — the covenant is not imposed but collectively ratified.
4: The systematic purge begins: all vessels made for Baal, Asherah, and the starry hosts are removed from the temple and burned at the Kidron Valley — the valley east of Jerusalem that served as the city’s ritual dump site (cf. 1 Kings 15:13; 2 Kings 23:6, 12). The ash is then carried to Bethel — sending the ash of Judah’s idols to the epicenter of Israel’s apostasy. The ʾidolatrous priests (kᵉmārîm) — a word used only for illegitimate cult personnel — are named for dismissal.
5: The kᵉmārîm — a contemptuous term for pagan priests — who burned incense (wayyaqqēṭer) at the high places in Judah’s towns are dismissed. The targets include the cults of the sun, moon, constellations (mazzālôt), and all the starry hosts — the full Mesopotamian astral pantheon Manasseh had introduced. Bāmôt (high places) from Jerusalem’s surroundings to Geba in the north and Beersheba in the south are defiled.
6: The Asherah pole is removed from the house of YHWH — the object Manasseh had placed inside the temple (2 Kings 21:7) — taken to the Kidron Valley, burned, ground to dust, and the dust scattered over the graves of common people. The five-step destruction sequence (remove, burn, grind to dust, scatter on graves) is the most thorough possible desecration of the cult object: the graves of the common people, being ritually defiling, contaminate whatever is scattered on them.
7: The quarters of the qᵉḏēšîm (weˀet-bāttê haqqᵉḏēšîm) are torn down — the male shrine prostitutes (qādēš, masculine of qᵉḏēšāh) whose quarters were inside the temple complex, where women wove hangings (bāttîm) for the Asherah. The combination of male cult prostitution and the Asherah weaving lodge in the temple precinct indicates how thoroughly the Solomonic temple had been converted to syncretistic use during Manasseh’s reign.
8: Josiah brings all the priests from the cities of Judah to Jerusalem and defiles (wayᵉṭammēʾ) the high places from Geba to Beersheba. The defilement is ritual: by bringing human bones or other impurity into contact with the high places, they are rendered unusable for worship. The centralization of the priesthood in Jerusalem follows Deuteronomy 12:5-7: all sacrifice is to happen at the single chosen place, not at local high places.
9: The priests from the defiled high places are not permitted to serve at the Jerusalem altar — there is a hierarchy of cultic purity even within the reform. They may eat unleavened bread among their priestly brothers but not serve at the altar. The matzot (unleavened bread) here likely refers to the priestly portion of offerings; the high-place priests are maintained but their altar service is permanently restricted. This nuanced approach — not executing them but restricting them — is a pastoral accommodation of existing cultic personnel.
10: “He desecrated (waytammēʾ) Topheth, which is in the Valley of Ben-Hinnom (gê ben hinnōm), so that no one could sacrifice his son or daughter in the fire to Molech.” Tōpet — the fire-altar site in the valley south of Jerusalem — was the location of child sacrifice to Molech, practiced by Manasseh (2 Kings 21:6) and apparently by many in Jerusalem (Jeremiah 7:31-32; 2 Kings 19:5-6). The gê ben hinnōm (Valley of Ben-Hinnom) will contract in later Hebrew to gêhinnōm — “Gehenna” — and become the NT’s primary term for the place of eschatological fire (Matthew 5:22, 29-30). Josiah’s defilement of the site is the historical action behind the name; the valley that was the site of infant burning becomes the image of divine judgment.
11: The horses dedicated to the sun (hassûsîm ʾăšer nātᵉnû malkê yᵉhûḏāh lāšemeš) — sun-worship in ANE religion involved ritual horse processions dedicated to the sun deity — are removed from the temple entrance and their solar chariots are burned. The astral cults involved elaborate cultic paraphernalia; the Assyrian sun-worship that Manasseh promoted included horse and chariot dedications documented in both biblical and extra-biblical texts.
12: Josiah pulls down the roof altars built by Ahaz (the ʿăliyyōt ʾāḥāz) and the altars built by Manasseh in the two courts of the temple. The accumulated idolatry of two previous kings is systematically demolished. The ashes are cast into the Kidron — the ritual dumping ground. The temple is being stripped back to its original Solomonic configuration.
13: “The king desecrated the high places that were east of Jerusalem on the south side of the Mount of Corruption (har hammašḥît) — the shrines Solomon king of Israel had built for Ashtoreth the detestable god of the Sidonians, for Chemosh the detestable god of Moab, and for Milcom the detestable god of the Ammonites.” The “Mount of Corruption” is the south ridge of the Mount of Olives — called har hammišḥāh (“mount of anointing”) in 2 Kings 11:7, now har hammašḥît (“mount of corruption/destruction”) — a wordplay that marks Solomon’s idolatrous high places as the corruption of what should have been a place of anointing. These shrines had stood for over 300 years since Solomon built them (1 Kings 11:7-8).
14: The sacred stones (maṣṣēḇôt) are smashed — the standing stones that functioned as Canaanite cultic markers, prohibited by Deuteronomy 12:3 and Exodus 23:24. The Asherah poles (ʾăšērîm) are cut down. The sites are covered with human bones (ʿaṣāmôt) — the most powerful available defiling agent (Numbers 19:16). The systematic use of human bones to defile cultic sites is a form of permanent disqualification: a site covered with bones can never again be used for sacrifice.
15: Even the Bethel altar — the oldest and most symbolically powerful site of the northern apostasy, built by Jeroboam son of Nebat (1 Kings 12:28-29) — is demolished, burned, ground to dust, and the Asherah burned. The prophecy of the unnamed man of God from Judah (1 Kings 13:2) — who foretold that “a son named Josiah will be born to the house of David and will burn human bones on you” — is here fulfilled, approximately 300 years after it was spoken. The fulfillment demonstrates that YHWH’s prophetic word is not merely predictive but historically executive.
16: Josiah notices the tombs on the hillside and has bones taken from them and burned on the Bethel altar — defiling it with human bones exactly as the man of God had prophesied (“bones burned on it,” 1 Kings 13:2). The 300-year gap between prophecy and fulfillment is the narrator’s implicit argument for the reliability of the prophetic word: no human planner could have staged this.
17: Josiah inquires about a particular monument and is told it is the tomb of “the man of God who came from Judah and predicted these things which you have done against the altar at Bethel.” The irony is layered: the man of God whose bones were buried at Bethel was the prophet whose disobedience on the return journey led to his death (1 Kings 13:11-32), yet his prophecy was entirely accurate. The accuracy of the prophecy transcended the prophet’s personal failure — the word YHWH gave him was true regardless of his subsequent disobedience.
18: “Leave him alone; let no one disturb his bones.” Josiah preserves both the man of God’s tomb and the tomb of the Samarian prophet who buried him (1 Kings 13:31) — the two are protected together. The preservation honors the prophetic word that has just been fulfilled; the man of God’s bones are the prophetic marker of what has happened.
19: Josiah extends the reform to Samaria — the former northern kingdom now under Assyrian provincial administration — removing the shrines at the high places and doing “everything he had done at Bethel.” The reach of Josiah’s reform into the former northern territory is possible because Assyrian power is waning (Assyria falls to Babylon in 612 BCE); Josiah fills the political vacuum with the reform. The inclusion of Samarian high places represents the implicit reunification of Israel and Judah under the covenant standard.
20: Josiah slaughters all the priests of the northern high places on their own altars and burns human bones on them. The parallel to the 1 Kings 13:2 prophecy is now fully enacted. The slaughter of the high-place priests in Samaria is more severe than the treatment of Judean high-place priests (v9: those were restricted but not killed) — the northern apostasy, being longer and more entrenched, receives harsher treatment.
21: “The king commanded all the people: Celebrate the Passover to YHWH your God, as it is written in this Book of the Covenant.” The Passover command closes the reform cycle: the book was found (2 Kings 22), the covenant was renewed (2 Kings 23:1-3), the cultic apparatus of apostasy was dismantled (2 Kings 23:4-20), and now the foundational festival is restored. Passover is the feast that defines Israel as the redeemed people of YHWH (Exodus 12:14); its restoration after a long lapse is the resumption of the foundational covenantal identity.
22: “Not since the days of the judges who led Israel, nor throughout the entire period of the kings of Israel and the kings of Judah, had any such Passover been celebrated.” The scope of the comparison is extraordinary: from the Judges through the entire united and divided monarchy, no Passover of this magnitude had been kept. The superlative marks Josiah’s Passover as the culmination of the covenant renewal — the most complete single act of covenant restoration in Israel’s history.
23: The specification: “in the eighteenth year of King Josiah.” The eighteenth year is the year of the book’s discovery (2 Kings 22:3) — the covenant renewal and the Passover restoration are accomplished in the same year. The eighteenth year is the theological center of the entire Josiah narrative: it is the year in which YHWH’s Torah is recovered and Israel’s foundational festival is restored.
24: Josiah removes the mediums, spiritists, household gods (ṭərāpîm), idols (gillûlîm), and all detestable things seen in Judah and Jerusalem — completing the list from Manasseh’s reign (2 Kings 21:6). The ṭərāpîm (household gods) are among the oldest and most persistent forms of Israelite syncretism (Genesis 31:19-35; Judges 17:5; 1 Samuel 19:13; Ezekiel 21:21). The comprehensiveness of the list signals that Josiah has reversed everything Manasseh instituted, matching item for item.
25: “Neither before nor after Josiah was there a king like him who turned to YHWH with all his heart (bəkol lᵉḇāḇô), with all his soul (bəkol napšô), and with all his might (ûḇəkol mᵉʾōḏô), in accordance with all the Law of Moses.” The three-part phrase is the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:5) applied to covenantal faithfulness. The superlative is total: Josiah is the king who most fully embodied the Shema. The comparison includes “before” — Josiah exceeds even David. Yet the following verse begins with the devastating ʾak — “nevertheless.”
26: “Nevertheless (ʾak), YHWH did not turn away from the burning of his great anger, which blazed against Judah because of all that Manasseh had done to provoke him.” The ʾak is the most devastating single word in the Josiah narrative: after the superlative praise of v25, it introduces the irreversibility of the divine judgment. Manasseh’s sin was not merely great but structurally final — it set in motion a judgment that even the best subsequent king could not reverse. The innocent blood of v21:16 is specifically named in 24:4 as the sin that YHWH “was not willing to pardon.”
27: “So YHWH said, ‘I will remove Judah from my sight as I removed Israel. I will reject Jerusalem, the city I chose, and this temple, about which I said, “There shall my Name be.”’” The divine speech cancels the election promise explicitly: the city chosen, the temple named — both will be rejected. The māʾas (reject) applied to Jerusalem echoes the rejection of Saul (1 Samuel 15:23, 26; 2 Kings 16:1) and Israel (2 Kings 17:20). YHWH’s māʾas is always the consequence of the covenant partner’s prior abandonment of the relationship.
28: Josiah’s regnal summary and reference to the annals. The standard closing formula marks the end of the Josiah account. The disaster of Megiddo (v29-30) falls outside the formal regnal summary, indicating it is epilogic rather than central to the narrator’s theological evaluation.
29: Pharaoh Necho of Egypt marches to the Euphrates to support Assyria against the rising Babylonian empire. Josiah goes to intercept him at Megiddo — why is unstated. The possibilities: pro-Babylonian strategy, territorial defense, or misguided messianic zeal. Necho kills him at Megiddo. The terseness of the Hebrew — wayyᵉmîtēhû ḇᵉmegiddô kᵉrᵉʾōtô, “he killed him at Megiddo when he encountered him” — is stark: the greatest covenant king dies in three words. Megiddo (later Harmāgᵉdôn in Revelation 16:16) is the place where Canaan’s forces were defeated by Deborah and Barak (Judges 5:19) and where Ahaziah died (2 Kings 9:27); it accumulates military-eschatological significance.
30: Josiah’s body is brought from Megiddo to Jerusalem by his servants in a chariot and buried in his own tomb. The ʿam hāʾāreṣ — the people of the land, who had made him king after Amon’s assassination — anoint his son Jehoahaz. The same constitutional actors who preserved the Davidic line after Amon’s coup (2 Kings 21:24) now install the new king after Josiah’s unexpected death. The burial in Jerusalem, in his own tomb, fulfills Huldah’s oracle: “you will be gathered to your ancestors” (2 Kings 22:20) — he dies before the exile.
31: Jehoahaz, twenty-three years old, reigns three months. The brevity — the same as Amon’s — signals the rapid unraveling of everything Josiah built. His mother Hamutal, daughter of Jeremiah of Libnah, is noted — a different Jeremiah from the prophet. The maternal genealogy is maintained even in the post-Josiah collapse.
32: “He did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, just as his predecessors had done.” The formula reverts to the pre-Josiah standard, applied after a single verse of introduction. Josiah’s reform has not taken root in his sons. The covenantal inheritance depends on the heart, not the institution; Josiah’s personal Shema-faithfulness did not transmit to his heirs.
33: Pharaoh Necho imprisons Jehoahaz at Riblah in Hamath and imposes a tribute of one hundred talents of silver and a talent of gold on the land. Riblah in Hamath will become the place of judgment for the last Judean kings (2 Kings 25:6, 20-21). Egypt asserts tributary control over Judah; the political independence of the Davidic monarchy is functionally over.
34: Necho installs Eliakim son of Josiah as king and renames him Jehoiakim. The name-change (ʿāzaḇ... wayyāśem) from ʾElyāqîm (“God establishes”) to Yəhôyāqîm (“YHWH establishes”) is a marker of Egyptian authority over the divine naming of Judah’s king: Necho substitutes the divine name in the king’s name as an act of ownership. The exiling of Jehoahaz to Egypt, where he dies (Jeremiah 22:11-12), removes the ʿam hāʾāreṣ’s chosen king permanently.
35: Jehoiakim taxes the land to pay Necho’s tribute — requiring each person to contribute according to their assessed wealth (kᵉʿerkô). The tribute system (100 talents silver, 1 talent gold) is extracted from the population rather than from the royal treasury, shifting the burden of imperial vassalage to the people. The same administrative extraction that Solomon used (1 Kings 4:7) returns under Egyptian compulsion.
36: Jehoiakim’s regnal notice: twenty-five years old, eleven-year reign. The eleven years spans the transition from Egyptian to Babylonian dominance (605 BCE: Battle of Carchemish). His mother Zebidah daughter of Pedaiah of Rumah is noted — the maternal genealogy continues.
37: “He did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, just as his predecessors had done.” The final verse of chapter 23 closes the chapter on the same formula that opened Jehoahaz’s brief reign. The post-Josiah collapse is complete: the two sons who follow him both receive the standard condemnation. The last word of chapter 23 in Hebrew is ʾăḇōtāyw — “his fathers” — the kings who preceded him. Josiah’s reform has left no successor; the “fathers” have triumphed over the son.
